posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
saw an offer yesterday that offered both of these from 2019 at different prices.

what is the difference?

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
saw an offer yesterday that offered both of these from 2019 at different prices.

what is the difference?

different plots of land. But all controlled and made by Rouget

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
saw an offer yesterday that offered both of these from 2019 at different prices.

what is the difference?

different plots of land. But all controlled and made by Rouget

ahhh. thank you. is one meant to be better? a lot better?

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
saw an offer yesterday that offered both of these from 2019 at different prices.

what is the difference?

different plots of land. But all controlled and made by Rouget

ahhh. thank you. is one meant to be better? a lot better?

As I’m not a huge fan of Rouget I’ve only tasted them side by side a handful of times in recent vintages. I can’t speak to that big of a difference.

I always thought Henri Jayer’s bottlings of his own Ech were better than the wines he made with his brother’s vines, but not significantly so and could also just be attributed to label bias on my part. Rouget now how his own Ech vines that he blends with the ones he got from Henri and he keeps the George separate. (I wonder if that’s what drives the price difference?)

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
different plots of land. But all controlled and made by Rouget

ahhh. thank you. is one meant to be better? a lot better?

As I’m not a huge fan of Rouget I’ve only tasted them side by side a handful of times in recent vintages. I can’t speak to that big of a difference.

I always thought Henri Jayer’s bottlings of his own Ech were better than the wines he made with his brother’s vines, but not significantly so and could also just be attributed to label bias on my part. Rouget now how his own Ech vines that he blends with the ones he got from Henri and he keeps the George separate. (I wonder if that’s what drives the price difference?)

I think the price difference is the label.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked: so nick, your take is that they are qualitatively equal?

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
so nick, your take is that they are qualitatively equal?

Not necessarily. But I do attribute a large part of the difference to label. In the end they’re made the same and from similar terroir.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
per jasper morris first book:


Echézeaux Grand Cru

The vines come from three sectors, Les Treux, Clos St-Denis and Les Cruots. Rouget’s Echézeaux appears in the market under two
slightly different labels, his own and another which refers to Georges Jayer.

i got the georges one - label isn’t as pretty.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
per jasper morris first book:


Echézeaux Grand Cru

The vines come from three sectors, Les Treux, Clos St-Denis and Les Cruots. Rouget’s Echézeaux appears in the market under two
slightly different labels, his own and another which refers to Georges Jayer.

i got the georges one - label isn’t as pretty.

I was under the impression that when Henri made the wines they were from different parcels. This makes it sound like all the parcels are blended.

@William Kelley any insight?

I have only ever tasted one Echézeaux in the Rouget cellar, and given the number of barrels, while it’s conceivable there are two different cuvées, I wouldn’t be surprised if they were one single vinification bottled under different labels.
